desc: Beppu is Kyushu's classic hot-spring base, where steaming neighborhoods, sea views, and short mountain escapes make it easy to build a wellness-first trip. It fits travelers who want traditional ryokan culture and regional food without Tokyo-scale logistics, especially when paired with Yufuin or Kunisaki day trips. Constraints are mostly practical: limited direct international access, strict ryokan meal times, and weather disruptions during the June rain season and late-summer typhoon spells.
hls: Kannawa's jigoku (hells) cluster including Umi Jigoku and Chinoike Jigoku geothermal sites
Takegawara Onsen and Kannawa lanes for sand baths, public baths, and classic ryokan streetscapes
Mt. Tsurumi Ropeway viewpoints over Beppu Bay with seasonal bloom and autumn foliage panoramas
Jigoku-mushi steam cuisine around Kannawa using geothermal vents for seafood and vegetables

todo.name: Complete the Beppu Seven Hells loop
todo.desc: Start when gates open and follow the Kannawa-to-Shibaseki route to see cobalt, red, and mud geothermal pools before tour crowds.

todo.name: Takegawara sand bath and retro bathhouse circuit
todo.desc: Book a sand bath at Takegawara Onsen, then continue to nearby neighborhood sento for contrasting mineral waters.

todo.name: Cook a jigoku-mushi lunch in Kannawa
todo.desc: Steam local vegetables, pork, and shellfish in geothermal vents at a dedicated jigoku-mushi workshop venue.

todo.name: Ride Mt. Tsurumi Ropeway and summit walk
todo.desc: Take the ropeway for bay views and do the short summit circuit for shrine stops and seasonal flowers.

todo.name: Day trip to Yufuin via JR or Yufuin no Mori route
todo.desc: Visit Kinrinko Lake and café streets in Yufuin, then return to Beppu for evening baths.

todo.name: Evening onsen-hopping in Kannawa
todo.desc: Walk lantern-lit steam alleys, try multiple small baths, and finish with local chicken tempura or toriten.

wifi.note: City hotels are dependable; smaller ryokan may have weaker room-level coverage.

prac.fltNote: There are no nonstop UK/Ireland-Oita services: most itineraries use one long-haul connection into Japan/Korea/Taiwan plus a domestic leg or rail transfer (common hubs: Tokyo Haneda/Narita, Osaka Kansai/Itami, Seoul Incheon, Taipei). Seat competition and fares generally rise around late March-April blossom travel, Golden Week (late April-early May), August Obon, and late-autumn foliage weekends. For flexibility, many travelers fly into Fukuoka and continue 2-2.5 hours by rail or highway bus to Beppu.
prac.lang: Japanese is the primary language; English support is best at transport hubs, hotels, and major attractions.
prac.tz: JST (UTC+9) year-round; no daylight saving time.
prac.visa: Japan entry is nationality-dependent; many short-stay tourists are visa-exempt, while others need a visa issued before departure.
alerts: Many smaller bathhouses and some ryokan still apply tattoo restrictions or cover-up rules, so confirm policy before booking private/public baths.
Ryokan kaiseki dinners often have hard cut-off times (commonly early evening), making late-arrival check-ins risky without prior notice.
June rain-front weeks and August-September typhoon systems can suspend ferries, ropeways, and regional trains at short notice.

sweet: Late October-November and March-May balance comfortable temperatures with strong onsen and day-trip conditions; June is greener but rainier, while August is hottest and pricier.
